divergences.description: Agent C classifies CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md operational exposure as IMPORTANT (part of internal ops runbook). Agent B classifies it as a separate BLOCKER due to specific GitHub App IDs and Modal secret naming patterns. Agent A folds it into a combined internal-docs BLOCKER. The disagreement is whether operational identifiers (not secrets) warrant BLOCKER status.

Agent B covers 19 issues. Agent A covers 12. Agent C covers approximately 10-11. All agents agree on the top ~10 findings, but Agent B uniquely covers CORS, security headers, dependency audit automation, print statement, .claude directory commands, and CODEOWNERS. These are real findings omitted by the others.

divergences.topic: Cosmetic branding as CRITICAL vs lower
divergences.description: Agent B classifies cosmetic pymc-labs references (example commands in HowItWorks, AnimatedTerminal) as CRITICAL Category C. Agent C treats branding references as acceptable and only flags deploy-breaking references. Agent A classifies all branding as CRITICAL. The disagreement is whether fork UX confusion from example code warrants CRITICAL.

rationale: Agent A produced the most effective round 01 revision by synthesizing all three agents' findings into a clean, unified set with zero remaining factual disagreements. While narrower than Agent B (12 vs 19 issues), Agent A covers every high-priority finding and correctly classifies all blockers. Agent A's explicit 'no disagreements' stance reflects genuine convergence on the core issues. Agent C made the strongest analytical improvements (release contract question, .env.example reclassification, expanded internal docs scope) but still omits several legitimate findings. The remaining divergences are about coverage depth and severity edge cases, not core blockers — all three agents now agree on the essential blocker set.
